name: Mock Test - 07
instruction: This question paper contains three sections – A, B and C. Each part is compulsory. Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20. 3 . Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10. There is no negative marking. All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question_text: let A = R - (3) and B = R - 1 Then, f : A {tex}\rightarrow B: f(x)=\frac{(x-2)}{(x-3)} \text { is }{/tex}

questions.question_text: The corner points of the feasible region determined by the system of linear constraints are (0, 0), (0, 40), (20, 40), (60, 20), (60, 0). The objective function is Z = 4x + 3y. Compare the quantity in Column A and Column B: Column A Column B Maximum of Z 325
questions.question_html:
The corner points of the feasible region determined by the system of linear constraints are (0, 0), (0, 40), (20, 40), (60, 20), (60, 0). The objective function is Z = 4x + 3y. Compare the quantity in Column A and Column B:

Column A Column B
Maximum of Z 325

options.text: The quantity in column B is greater
options.html: The quantity in column B is greater

options.text: The quantity in column A is greater
options.html: The quantity in column A is greater

options.text: The relationship can not be determined on the basis of the information supplied
options.html: The relationship can not be determined on the basis of the information supplied

questions.correct_option_text: The quantity in column B is greater
questions.explanation: Corner Points Corresponding Value of Z = 4x + 3y (0, 0) 0 (0, 40) 120 (20, 40) 200 (60, 20) 300 (Maximum) (60, 0) 240 So, maximum value of Z = 300 < 325. Therefore, the quantity in column B is greater. Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: If y = ax n+1 + bx -n , then {tex}x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}={/tex}
questions.question_html: If y = axn+1 + bx-n, then {tex}x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}={/tex}
questions.explanation: Here, y = ax n+1 + bx -n {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = a (n + 1) x n - bn x -n-1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} = a n.(n + 1) x n-1 + b n.(n + 1) x -n-2 } {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x^2\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} = x 2 {a n (n + 1) x n-1 + b n (n + 1) x n-2 } = n (n + 1) (ax n+1 + bx -n ) = n (n + 1)y

questions.correct_option_text: Find the feasible region of the linear programming problem and determine its corner points (vertices).
questions.explanation: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem the first step is : To find the feasible region of the linear programming problem and determine its corner points (vertices) either by inspection or by solving the two equations of the lines intersecting at that point.

questions.question_text: f(x) = sin x is increasing in
questions.question_html: f(x) = sin x is increasing in
questions.explanation: {tex}\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Given function,f(x) is sin x {tex}\therefore {/tex} f'(x) = cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = cos x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} for {tex}x \in\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} f'(x) is increasing,we {tex}\therefore {/tex} f(x) is increasing in {tex}\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} . Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: If the ratio of base radius and height of a cone is 1:2 and percentage error in radius is x%, then the error in its volume is
questions.question_html: If the ratio of base radius and height of a cone is 1:2 and percentage error in radius is x%, then the error in its volume is
questions.explanation: Given that the radius is half than the height of the cone so Suppose h = 2x (where x is radius and h is height of the cone) Volume of the cone = v {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \times \pi x^{2} \times h{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \times \pi x^{3}{/tex} (because h = 2x ) {tex}\Delta v=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \pi \times 3 x^{2} \Delta x{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} v = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} x 2 {tex}\Delta{/tex} x Therefore finally , we obtain {tex}\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}}\right) \times 100=3 \cdot\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \times 100{/tex} = 3 {tex}\lambda{/tex} %

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \infty } \;{\left( {\;\frac{{2x + 3}}{{2x + 1}}} \right)^x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \infty } \;{\left( {\;\frac{{2x + 3}}{{2x + 1}}} \right)^x}{/tex}is equal to
questions.explanation: We know that, {tex}{\lim _{x \to \infty }}{(1 + \frac{a}{x})^x} = {e^a}{/tex} ....(1) Now, {tex}{\lim _{x \to \infty }}{\left( {\frac{{2x + 3}}{{2x + 1}}} \right)^x} = {\lim _{x \to \infty }}{\left( {1 + \frac{2}{{2x + 1}}} \right)^x} = {e^2},{/tex} by eq(1)

questions.question_html: If two corner points of the feasible region are both optimal solutions of the same type, i.e., both produce the same maximum or minimum.

options.text: then no point on the line segment joining these two points is an optimal solution of the same type
options.html: then no point on the line segment joining these two points is an optimal solution of the same type

options.text: then any point on the line segment joining these two points is also an optimal solution of the same type

options.html: then no point on the line segment joining these two points is an optimal solution of the opposite type

questions.correct_option_text: then any point on the line segment joining these two points is also an optimal solution of the same type
questions.explanation: If two corner points of the feasible region are both optimal solutions of the same type, i.e., both produce the same maximum or minimum , then any point on the line segment joining these two points is also an optimal solution of the same type .

questions.question_text: If the function f(x) = kx 3 - 9x 2 + 9 x + 3 is monotonically increasing in every interval, then
questions.question_html: If the function f(x) = kx3 - 9x2 + 9 x + 3 is monotonically increasing in every interval, then
questions.explanation: f(x) = kx 3 - 9x 2 + 9x + 3 f'(x) = 3kx 2 - 18x + 9 = 3(kx 2 - 6x + 3) Given: f(x) is monotonically increasing in every interval. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3(kx 2 - 6x + 3) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (kx 2 - 6x = 3) > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K > 0 and (-6) 2 - 4(k)(3) < 0 [ {tex}\because{/tex} ax 2 + bx + c > 0 and D is c < 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k > 0 and (-6) 2 - 4(k) (3) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k > 0 and 36 - 12k < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k > 0 and 12k > 36 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k > 0 and k > 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k > 3

questions.question_text: The function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{e^{1 / x}-1}{e^{1 / x}+1}, & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
questions.question_html: The function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{e^{1 / x}-1}{e^{1 / x}+1}, & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}

options.text: is not continuous at x = 0, but can be made continuous at x = 0
options.html: is not continuous at x = 0, but can be made continuous at x = 0

questions.explanation: Given: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{e^{1 / x}-1}{e^{1 / x}+1}, & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} We have {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{\frac{1}{x}}-1}{e^{\frac{1}{x}}+1}\right){/tex} If {tex}e^{\frac{1}{x}}=t,{/tex} then {tex}x \rightarrow 0, t \rightarrow \infty{/tex} Also, f(0) = 0 {tex}\therefore \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} f(x) \neq f(0){/tex} Hence, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 0

questions.question_text: If {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1-\sin ^{2} x}{3 \cos ^{2} x} & , & x<\frac{\pi}{2} \\ a & , & x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{b(1-\sin x)}{(\pi-2 x)^{2}} & , & x>\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Then, f(x) is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , if
questions.question_html: If {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1-\sin ^{2} x}{3 \cos ^{2} x} & , & x<\frac{\pi}{2} \\ a & , & x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{b(1-\sin x)}{(\pi-2 x)^{2}} & , & x>\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Then, f(x) is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, if
questions.explanation: Let f(x) be continuous at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Then LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{(\frac{\pi }{2})}^ - }} \frac{{1 - {{\sin }^2}x}}{{3{{\cos }^2}x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{(\frac{\pi }{2})}^ - }} \frac{{{{\cos }^2}x}}{{3{{\cos }^2}x}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Again, RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{(\frac{\pi }{2})}^ + }} \frac{{b(1 - \sin x)}}{{{{(\pi - 2x)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{b(1 - \sin (\frac{\pi }{2} + h))}}{{{{[\pi - 2(\frac{\pi }{2} + h)]}^2}}}{/tex} = b {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{(1 - \cos \,h)}}{{{{[\pi - \pi - 2h)}^2}}}{/tex} = b {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{(1 - \cos \,h)}}{{4{h^2}}}{/tex} = b {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}\frac{h}{2}}}{{\frac{{{h^2}}}{4} \times {4^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{2b}{16}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b}{8}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = {tex}\frac{8}{3}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If log e 4 = 1.3868, then log e 4.01 =
questions.question_html: If loge 4 = 1.3868, then loge 4.01 =
questions.explanation: let y = f(x) = logx Let x = 4, X + {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 4.01, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 0.01, For x = 4, y = log4 = 1.3868, y = logx {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Δy = dy {tex}=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \cdot d x{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) \times 0.01{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} y = 0.0025 So, log(4.01) = y + {tex}\Delta{/tex} y = 1.3893

questions.question_text: If y = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{1-\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = tan-1 {tex}\left(\frac{1-\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given that y = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} Using 1 - cos x = 2sin 2 {tex}\frac {x} {2}{/tex} and Using sin x = 2 sin x {tex}\frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}{/tex} , we obtain y = {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}\right){/tex} or y = {tex}\tan ^{-1} \tan \frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}y=\frac{x}{2}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The value of tan -1 {tex}\left(\tan \frac{7 \pi}{6}\right){/tex} is
questions.question_html: The value of tan-1 {tex}\left(\tan \frac{7 \pi}{6}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation: Let x = tan -1 {tex}\left(tan \frac{7 \pi}{6}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = tan {tex}\left(\frac{7 \pi}{6}\right){/tex} Here the range of principle value of tan is {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{7 \pi}{6} \notin\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} Therefore for all values of x in range {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} , the value of tan -1 {tex}\left(tan \frac{7 \pi}{6}\right){/tex} is {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = tan {tex}\left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} tan {tex}\left(\frac{7 \pi}{6}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = tan {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} tan {tex}(\pi + \theta){/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{X}=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: An edge of a variable cube is increasing at the rate of 3cm/sec. Find the rate at which the volume of the cube is increasing when the edge is 10cm long.
questions.question_html: An edge of a variable cube is increasing at the rate of 3cm/sec. Find the rate at which the volume of the cube is increasing when the edge is 10cm long.
questions.explanation: Let the side of the cube is x Given {tex} \frac { dx }{ dt } =3cm/sec{/tex} We have volume of a cube = V = x 3 Now, {tex}\frac { dV }{ dt } =\frac { d }{ dt } \left( { x }^{ 3 } \right) =\frac { d }{ dx } \left( { x }^{ 3 } \right) \frac { dx }{ dt } =3{ x }^{ 2 }.\frac { dx }{ dt } =9{ x }^{ 2 }{/tex} When x = 10 cm then {tex}\frac { dV }{ dt } =\quad 9.{ \left( 10 \right) }^{ 2 }=900{ cm }^{ 3 }/sec{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \infty } \;{\left( {1 + \frac{3}{x}} \right)^x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \infty } \;{\left( {1 + \frac{3}{x}} \right)^x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } {\left( {1 + \frac{3}{x}} \right)^x} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{t \to {0^ + }} {\left( {1 + 3t} \right)^{\frac{1}{t}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{3t \to {0^ + }} {\left[ {{{\left( {1 + 3t} \right)}^{\frac{1}{{3t}}}}} \right]^3} = {e^3}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The value of {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} is
questions.question_html: The value of {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation: {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(2 \pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}+\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \left(2 \pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}+\sin ^{-1}\left(-\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}-\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} = 0

questions.question_text: If R is a relation from a non – empty set A to a non – empty set B, then
questions.question_html: If R is a relation from a non – empty set A to a non – empty set B, then
questions.explanation: Let A and B be two sets. Then a relation R from set A to set B is a subset of A×B.Thus, R is a relation from A to B {tex} \Leftrightarrow R \subseteq A \times B{/tex} .

questions.question_text: The distance moved by the particle in time t is given by {tex}x=t^{3}-12 t^{2}+6 t+8{/tex} . At the instant when its acceleration is zero, the velocity is
questions.question_html: The distance moved by the particle in time t is given by {tex}x=t^{3}-12 t^{2}+6 t+8{/tex}. At the instant when its acceleration is zero, the velocity is
questions.explanation: {tex}x=t^{3}-12 t^{2}+6 t+8{/tex} Differentiating with respect to t, {tex}v=\frac{d x}{d t}=3 t^{2}-24 t+6{/tex} {tex}a=\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=6 t-24{/tex} Given that a = 0 {tex}a=\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=6 t-24{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 t-24=0 \Rightarrow t=4{/tex} Velocity v at t = 4, {tex}v=\frac{d x}{d t}=3 t^{2}-24 t+6{/tex} {tex}v=3 \times 4^{2}-24 \times 4+6{/tex} v = - 42

questions.question_text: If x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R, then the determinant {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\cos x} & {-\sin x} & {1} \\ {\sin x} & {\cos x} & {1} \\ {\cos (x+y)} & {-\sin (x+y)} & {0} \end{array}\right|{/tex} is in:
questions.question_html: If x, y {tex}\in{/tex}  R, then the determinant {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\cos x} & {-\sin x} & {1} \\ {\sin x} & {\cos x} & {1} \\ {\cos (x+y)} & {-\sin (x+y)} & {0} \end{array}\right|{/tex} is in:
questions.explanation: Applying R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 3 – cos y R 1 + sin y R 2 , we get {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\cos x} & {-\sin x} & {1} \\ {\sin x} & {\cos x} & {1} \\ {0} & {0} & {\sin y-\cos y} \end{array}\right|{/tex} Expanding along R 3 , we have {tex}\Delta{/tex} = (siny – cosy) (cos 2 x + sin 2 x) {tex}=(\sin y-\cos y){/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin y-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos y\right]{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{4} \sin y-\sin \frac{\pi}{4} \cos y\right]{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2} \sin \left(y-\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} Therefore,the required answer is {tex}-\sqrt{2} \leq \Delta \leq \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.correct_option_text: continuous at x = 0 as well as at x = 1.
questions.explanation: continuous at x = 0 as well as at x = 1.

questions.question_text: If {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} a x^{2}+b & ,\ \ \ 0 \leq x<1 \\ 4 & , \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \quad x=1 \\ x+3 & , \quad 1<x \leq 2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} then the value of (a, b) for which f(x) cannot be continuous at x = 1, is
questions.question_html: If {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} a x^{2}+b & ,\ \ \ 0 \leq x<1 \\ 4 & , \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \quad x=1 \\ x+3 & , \quad 1<x \leq 2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} then the value of (a, b) for which f(x) cannot be continuous at x = 1, is
questions.explanation: If f(x) is continuous at x = 1, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=f(1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(1-h)=4{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} f(1) = 4] {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} a(1-h)^{2}+b=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a + b) = 4 Thus, the possible values of (a, b) can be (2, 2), (3, 1), (4, 0) but (a, b) {tex}\neq{/tex} (5, 2) Hence, for (a, b) = (5, 2) f(x) cannot be continuous at x = 1

questions.question_text: If x lies in the interval [0, 1], then the least value of x 2 + x + 1 is
questions.question_html: If x lies in the interval [0, 1], then the least value of x2 + x + 1 is
questions.explanation: f(x) = x 2 + x + 1 f'(x) = 2x + 1 To find minima f'(x) = 0 2x + 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \notin{/tex} [0, 1] f(0) = 0 and f(1) = 3 > 0 Hence, at x = 1 function has minima.

questions.question_text: The principal value of cosec -1 {tex}\left(cosec (\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right)){/tex} is
questions.question_html: The principal value of cosec-1 {tex}\left(cosec (\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right)){/tex} is
questions.explanation: Let x = cosec -1 {tex}\left(cosec (\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right)){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec x = cosec {tex}\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} Here range of principle value of cosec is {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{4 \pi}{3} \notin\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} Hence, for all values of x in range {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} , the value of cosec -1 {tex}\left(cosec (\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right)){/tex} is {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec x = cosec {tex}\left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} cosec {tex}\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} = cosec {tex}\left(\pi+\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cosec x = cosec {tex}\left(-\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} cosec {tex}(\pi+\theta){/tex} = cosec ( {tex}-\theta{/tex} )) {tex}\Rightarrow x=-\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The feasible region for a LPP is shown in Figure. Evaluate Z = 4x + y at each of the corner points of this region. Find the minimum value of Z, if it exists
questions.question_html: The feasible region for a LPP is shown in Figure. Evaluate Z = 4x + y at each of the corner points of this region. Find the minimum value of Z, if it exists
questions.explanation: Corner points Z = 4x + y (0, 2 ) 2 (0,3) 3 (2,1) 9 Hence the minimum value is 2

questions.question_text: If the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3-2 x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} is singular then x = ?.
questions.question_html: If the matrix {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3-2 x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} is singular then x = ?.
questions.explanation: When a given matrix is singular then the given matrix determinant is 0. |A| = 0 Given, {tex}A=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 3-2 x & x+1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{array}\right) {/tex} |A| = 0 4(3 - 2x) - 2(x + 1) = 0 12 - 8x - 2x - 2 = 0 10 - 10x = 0 10(1 - x) = 0 x = 1

questions.question_text: The tangent to the curve given by x = e t {tex}\times{/tex} cost, y = e t {tex}\times{/tex} sin t at t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} makes with x-axis an angle:
questions.question_html: The tangent to the curve given by x = et {tex}\times{/tex} cost, y = et {tex}\times{/tex} sin t at t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} makes with x-axis an angle:
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}=-e^{t} \cdot \sin t+e^{t} \cos t, {/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=e^{t} \cos t+e^{t} \sin t{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{t=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{\cos t+\sin t}{\cos t-\sin t}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{0}{/tex} and hence this option is correct

questions.question_text: If x p y q = (x +y) p+q , then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If  xp yq = (x +y)p+q, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: Given, x p y q = (x +y) p+q Taking log on both sides we get: plogx + q logy = (p + q)log(x + y). Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x we get , {tex}\begin{gathered} \frac{p}{x} + \frac{q}{y}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\end{gathered} {/tex} = (p + q) {tex}\left( {\frac{1}{{x + y}}} \right)\left( {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{q}{y} - \frac{{p + q}}{{x + y}}} \right)= \frac{{p + q}}{{x + y}} - \frac{p}{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\left[\frac{x\left(p+q\right)-p\left(x+y\right)}{x\left(x+y\right)}\right]\left[\frac{y\left(x+y\right)}{q\left(x+y\right)-y\left(p+q\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac yx{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R be given by f (x) = [x] 2 + [x + 1] - 3, where [x] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x. Then, f (x) is
questions.question_html: Let f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R be given by f (x) = [x]2 + [x + 1] - 3, where [x] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x. Then, f (x) is
questions.explanation: Given that f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R be given by f(x) = [x] 2 + [x + 1] - 3 As [x] is the greatest integer so for different values of x, we will get same value of f(x). [x] 2 + [x + 1] will always be an integer. So, f is many-one. Similarly, in this function co domain is mapped with at most one element of domain because for every x {tex}\in{/tex} R, f(x) {tex}\in{/tex} Z. So, f is into.

questions.question_text: If tan -1 3x + tan -1 2x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} then x = ?
questions.question_html: If tan-1 3x + tan-1 2x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} then x = ?
questions.explanation: It is given : tan -1 3x + tan -1 2x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} We have to find: The value of x Since we know that, tan -1 x + tan -1 y = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{x+y}{1-x y}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 3x + tan -1 2x = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{3 x+2 x}{1-(3 x \times 2 x)}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5 x}{1-6 x^{2}}\right){/tex} Now since tan -1 3x + tan -1 2x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} tan -1 3x + tan -1 2x = tan -1 1 ( {tex}\because{/tex} tan {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 ( {tex}\frac{5 x}{1-6 x^{2}}{/tex} ) = tan -1 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5 x}{1-6 x^{2}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 x 2 + 5x - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} or x = -1. Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: Let A and B be independent events with P(A) = 0.3 and P(B) = 0.4. Find P(A ∪ B).
questions.question_html: Let A and B be independent events with P(A) = 0.3 and P(B) = 0.4. Find P(A ∪ B).
questions.explanation: Let A and B be independent events with P (A) = 0.3 and P(B) = 0.4 Since the events are independent, P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = P(A) {tex}\cdot{/tex} P(B) Therefore P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = P(A) + P(B) = 0.3 + 0.4 - 0.12 = 0.58

They sold handmade fans, mats, and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹ 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold by school A, B, C are given below. Article School A B C Fans 40 25 35 Mats 50 40 50 Plates 20 30 40 If P be a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix represent the sale of handmade fans, mats and plates by three schools A, B and C, then
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Three schools A, B and C organized a mela for collecting funds for helping the rehabilitation of flood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats, and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹ 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold by school A, B, C are given below.

Article School A B C
Fans 40 25 35
Mats 50 40 50
Plates 20 30 40

If P be a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 matrix represent the sale of handmade fans, mats and plates by three schools A, B and C, then
questions.explanation: Clearly, P = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}&&Fans&Mats&Plates\end{array}}{\begin{array}{c}A\\B\\C\end{array}\begin{bmatrix}40&50&20\\25&40&30\\35&50&40\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 46 to 50: Three schools A, B and C organized a mela for collecting funds for helping the rehabilitation of flood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats, and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹ 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold by school A, B, C are given below. Article School A B C Fans 40 25 35 Mats 50 40 50 Plates 20 30 40 The funds collected by school A by selling the given articles is
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Three schools A, B and C organized a mela for collecting funds for helping the rehabilitation of flood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats, and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹ 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold by school A, B, C are given below.

Article School A B C
Fans 40 25 35
Mats 50 40 50
Plates 20 30 40

The funds collected by school A by selling the given articles is
questions.explanation: Clearly, total funds collected by each school is given by the matrix PQ = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 40 & 50 & 20 \\ 25 & 40 & 30 \\ 35 & 50 & 40 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 25 \\ 100 \\ 50 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 1000+5000+1000 \\ 625+4000+1500 \\ 875+5000+2000 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 7000 \\ 6125 \\ 7875 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Funds collected by school A is ₹ 7000 Funds collected by school B is ₹ 6125 Funds collected by school C is ₹ 7875

Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Three schools A, B and C organized a mela for collecting funds for helping the rehabilitation of flood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats, and plates from recycled material at a cost of ₹ 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold by school A, B, C are given below.

Article School A B C
Fans 40 25 35
Mats 50 40 50
